entities.description: The anonymous Middle English prose dialogue as an intellectual work, known only in an acephalous state from Stowe MS 34. This abstract boundary separates the medieval composition from its physical witness, scholarly editions, translations, digital encodings, and repository artifact.
entities.label: Vices and Virtues as an abstract anonymous work

rights.basis: The work was composed and copied around 1200; no modern intellectual contribution is included in this component.
rights.component: The anonymous medieval Middle English and embedded Latin textual work

The repository assertion is therefore recorded for contributor-owned software and repository contributions without treating it as blanket permission for inherited material or as overriding the XML's embedded 1997 U-M redistribution terms.

Sencor keyboarded the text; the header reports five-percent proofing to an error rate of 1 in 20,000, Janet Erickson's markup, and Jason Chu's proof and markup review.

access.accessMethod: Use the current BL Archives and Manuscripts catalog record and cite current shelfmark Stowe MS 34.

No complete public facsimile was found in the current catalog, official digitised-manuscript list, or tested public endpoint patterns. Recheck the catalog and ask BL reference services before concluding that no internal or unindexed photography exists.
notes: Crawford's thesis supplies only two manuscript photographs, f. 18v and f. 43v.

access.accessMethod: Register for or renew a British Library Reader Pass, submit the collection-item request form using Stowe MS 34, and consult the manuscript in London. The catalog says a letter of introduction is required.

Submit the BL imaging order form and request a quote for full-color photography of the complete surviving object: every folio recto and verso plus boards, binding, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges, detached matter, and any housing or associated matter that can safely be photographed. Ask BL to confirm the current leaf inventory and do not restrict the order to ff. 1r-48r.
access.contact: permissions@bl.uk for reuse permission; licensing@bl.uk is also named on the digitisation service page
access.format: Rare-item photography; request color TIFF explicitly because original manuscripts cannot be scanned
notes: The 2025/26 price card lists photographic TIFF at GBP 27.70 for the first image and GBP 7.25 for each subsequent image. Ninety-six to ninety-eight folio-side images alone would nominally be GBP 716.45-730.95 before boards, flyleaves, blanks, housing, handling, or other charges; obtain a formal quote.
The work association remains ff. 1r-48r even though the requested reproduction should cover the entire surviving manuscript object.

rights.basis: BL imaging terms distinguish supply from reuse and require written permission for publication or other reuse; no item-specific image set or license has yet been supplied.
rights.component: Any manuscript photographs supplied or newly made by the British Library
rights.jurisdiction: British Library imaging terms and applicable territories
notes: Request written terms that separately address research use, repository redistribution, commercial and nonprofit use, print-on-demand use, attribution, modification, and sublicensing.
The underlying medieval work remains public domain; this record addresses only supplied image files and provider terms.

access.accessMethod: Ask BL Reference Services whether existing unindexed photography, legacy microfilm, conservation imaging, or other surrogates cover Stowe MS 34 and request confirmation of the complete folio/leaf inventory before ordering.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: Gunn describes the manuscript more narrowly as first quarter of the thirteenth century.

entities.description: The unique acephalous manuscript witness of Vices and Virtues, written on vellum in the early thirteenth century. The work occupies surviving fols. 1r-48r; its missing first eight-leaf quire contained the original opening. The current BL catalog describes 49 folios, while Crawford describes 48 surviving leaves in six gatherings of eight.

holding.notes: The former shelfmark is historically secure but collides with an unrelated current Stowe MS 240. Always request and cite current Stowe MS 34.

entities.label: British Library Stowe MS 34
notes: The work locus is ff. 1r-48r; Holthausen and U-M additionally map this as manuscript pages 1-95.
The BL Digitised Content field is blank and no entry for Stowe MS 34 appears in the current official digitised-manuscript list.

physicalDescription.dimensions: Approximately 22 x 16 cm according to Crawford
physicalDescription.extent: Current BL catalog: 49 folios; Crawford: 48 surviving leaves in six gatherings of eight
physicalDescription.format: Small quarto in eighteenth-century crimson morocco
notes: The beginning is wanting; Crawford reconstructs the lost first quire as eight leaves.
A strong change of hand is placed at f. 39 by the BL catalog and at f. 38r by Crawford, who says the second scribe fills f. 37v.
William Fleetwood's autograph appears on the last leaf, and Thomas Astle's name appears at the beginning.

rights.basis: The manuscript content was created in the early thirteenth century. Physical ownership, consultation rules, and rights or terms in modern photography remain separate layers.
rights.component: The medieval text, scribal writing, corrections, rubrication, and other historical manuscript content embodied in Stowe MS 34
notes: This record does not authorize access to the physical object or reuse of British Library photographs; those are covered separately.

next_steps: Ask BL Reference Services about every existing surrogate under both current Stowe MS 34 and former Stowe MS 240 identifiers.

If no suitable surrogate exists, request a quote for full-color photography of every surviving folio recto and verso plus boards, binding, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges, detached matter, and housing that can safely be imaged.
Ask BL to confirm the current complete inventory and do not limit the reproduction order to the work's ff. 1r-48r locus.
Obtain written terms for research, repository redistribution, commercial and nonprofit publication, print-on-demand, attribution, modification, and sublicensing before publishing any supplied image.
openQuestions.question: Does the British Library hold unindexed photography, conservation imaging, legacy microfilm, or another complete surrogate of Stowe MS 34, and what research and reuse terms would apply?

next_steps: Ask BL for the current foliation, collation, blank/flyleaf treatment, and item-level inventory.
Compare a complete facsimile against Crawford's collation and the catalog description.
Check whether BL's f. 39 change and Crawford's f. 38r takeover/f. 37v filling describe the same transition under different counting conventions.
Preserve both assertions until resolved.
openQuestions.question: Why does the current BL record describe 49 folios while Crawford describes 48 surviving leaves in six gatherings of eight, and exactly where does the principal hand change occur?

next_steps: Request written clarification from U-M Library that separately addresses copyright, provider terms, commercial and nonprofit redistribution, and reuse of encoding/markup.

Ask the CME maintainers whether the root Unlicense intentionally covers imported corpus XML, data, transcription, encoding, and markup, and what authority supports that scope rather than only contributor-owned software and repository additions.
Trace the artifact's import and modification history to distinguish inherited U-M material from any contributor-owned CME additions.
Until both providers clarify the scope, retain all three assertions without selecting whichever is most permissive or treating the CME LICENSE as an override.
Separate public-domain medieval and 1888 text from modern encoding structure in any reuse analysis.
openQuestions.question: How should three unresolved rights layers be reconciled: U-M's current No Copyright-United States display, the restrictive redistribution statement embedded in the 1997 XML, and CME's root Unlicense whose operative grant repeatedly refers to software? In particular, does U-M consider the 1997 statement superseded, and did CME intend or have authority for the Unlicense to cover the inherited U-M XML, data, transcription, encoding, or markup?

next_steps: Ask EETS or OUP which source master and preliminary matter the current product reproduces.
Physically inspect a current copy's title pages, copyright page, pagination, ISBN, and typographic identity.
Add a version_of or facsimile relation only after direct evidence identifies its ancestry.
openQuestions.question: Is the current ISBN 9780197222607 paperback a facsimile of the 1888 issue, the 1967 reprint, or another reset or print-on-demand master?

next_steps: Verify the call number in U-M's current library catalog and ask reference staff about onsite, lending, or reproduction access.
Request images of all preliminary pages and representative body pages if needed to verify the 1967 carrier against 1888.
Do not substitute the BSB 1888 scan or current backlist product for the exact 1967 source issue.
openQuestions.question: What is the current access and circulation status of the exact U-M source copy at call number 820.6 E13 no.89 1967, and does any public or requestable full facsimile of that carrier exist?

relations.subjectLabel: OUP 1967 reprint of Vices and Virtues, Part I

assertion.notes: EETS's exact backmatter wording calls OS 89 'Vices and Virtues, from the unique MS., ab. 1200 a.d., ed. Dr. F. Holthausen'; Part II's title says it was edited 'from the Stowe MS. 240'; and its notes and glossary repeatedly identify MS readings distinct from the printed text. Crawford's later direct comparison calls the edition sound and thorough with few errors. This supports a continuous transcribes edge but does not document Holthausen's precise autopsy or working-copy method.

scope.notes: The 1888 title uses the then-current shelfmark Stowe MS 240; this is the same physical witness now cataloged as Stowe MS 34.

reviewNotes: The 1967 OUP reprint is modeled as a distinct carrier/edition entity. Its version_of relation to 1888 is supported by its explicit reprint statement and matching body pagination, not inferred merely from a later catalog record.

The U-M selection boundary is explicit: the Middle English odd pages are encoded and the facing modern English translation is omitted. Translation language and rights do not silently transfer into the repository work representation.
Current Stowe MS 34 and former Stowe MS 240 designate this witness. Current Stowe MS 240 is an unrelated nineteenth-century transcript volume and must never be substituted for it.
The British Library's current 49-folio extent, Crawford's 48 surviving leaves, and the f. 38r/f. 39 hand-boundary descriptions remain unresolved parallel assertions.

Access and rights statements are entity- and component-specific. Public-domain medieval or nineteenth-century verbal content does not automatically settle rights or provider terms for U-M markup, BSB/Google scans, Crawford's thesis, Internet Archive derivatives, or future BL photography. The CME root Unlicense is recorded for contributor-owned software and repository contributions, but its software-worded grant is not treated as blanket permission for inherited U-M XML, data, or markup or as overriding the embedded 1997 terms.
Negative public-facsimile findings are date-bounded and do not prove that no unindexed, internal, private, or legacy photographic surrogate exists.

It does not expressly identify the imported U-M XML corpus, data, transcription, encoding, or markup as covered software, and no path-specific notice was found. The repository assertion is therefore recorded for contributor-owned software and repository contributions without treating it as blanket permission for inherited material or as overriding the XML's embedded 1997 U-M redistribution terms.

sourceLinks.accessMethod: Register for or renew a British Library Reader Pass, submit the collection-item request form using Stowe MS 34, and consult the manuscript in London. The catalog says a letter of introduction is required.

Submit the BL imaging order form and request a quote for full-color photography of the complete surviving object: every folio recto and verso plus boards, binding, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges, detached matter, and any housing or associated matter that can safely be photographed. Ask BL to confirm the current leaf inventory and do not restrict the order to ff. 1r-48r.
sourceLinks.contact: permissions@bl.uk for reuse permission; licensing@bl.uk is also named on the digitisation service page
sourceLinks.format: Rare-item photography; request color TIFF explicitly because original manuscripts cannot be scanned
notes: The 2025/26 price card lists photographic TIFF at GBP 27.70 for the first image and GBP 7.25 for each subsequent image. Ninety-six to ninety-eight folio-side images alone would nominally be GBP 716.45-730.95 before boards, flyleaves, blanks, housing, handling, or other charges; obtain a formal quote.
The work association remains ff. 1r-48r even though the requested reproduction should cover the entire surviving manuscript object.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned CME serialization of University of Michigan item VicesV1. U-M keyed a selection from the exact 1967 Oxford University Press reprint identified in its source call number: three reprint title pages and Holthausen's Middle English text on odd printed pages 3-151, but not the facing modern English translation on even pages 2-150. The 1967 issue explicitly reprints Ferdinand Holthausen's 1888 EETS Original Series 89 Part I, whose continuous Middle English text is identified by EETS as edited from the unique, acephalous witness now called British Library Stowe MS 34 and formerly called Stowe MS 240. Holthausen's 1921 Part II supplies notes and glossary but is not an ancestor of the U-M text. Judith Crawford's 1987 thesis is a later direct manuscript re-edition and includes only two selected manuscript photographs, not a complete facsimile. A complete public facsimile of Stowe MS 34 was not found in the current BL catalog, official digitised-manuscript list, or endpoint audit as of 2026-07-15; BL offers onsite consultation and fee-based photography. The complete 1888 Part I, complete 1921 Part II, complete Crawford thesis, official 2026 EETS list, and U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A have been cached locally as research evidence.

catalogingSubject.label: Vices and Virtues
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The surviving acephalous portion of an anonymous Middle English prose dialogue conventionally called Vices and Virtues, represented by British Library Stowe MS 34, fols. 1r-48r. The cataloged intellectual unit is not the complete Stowe codex, Holthausen's facing-page edition and translation, its 1967 reprint, Crawford's later re-edition, the University of Michigan digital selection, or the checked-in XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Surviving acephalous dialogue

locators.value: TEXT/BODY/DIV1[1]-DIV1[60]; 77 P, 75 PB, and MILESTONE UNIT='page' N='1'-'95'

Middle English text on odd pp. 3-151; facing modern English translation on even pp. 2-150 is not encoded

notes: The first eight-leaf quire is missing; neither the lost opening nor the likely earlier treatment of confession and principal sins is reconstructed here.
The sixty U-M sections preserve editorial/encoding structure and should not be treated as sixty independently authored works or canonical chapters.
contentParts.summary: The complete surviving portion from the mid-sentence extant opening through the prose conclusion and closing Latin hymn, including the Soul's confession and Reason's instruction on vices, faith, virtues, confession, restraint, and prayer.

assertion.notes: Crawford considers a date more than about fifty years before the manuscript unlikely; Gunn says composition was probably before 1200; MED uses a c1200 citation stencil. No internal composition date is known.
The broad normalized range follows the current British Library catalog; Gunn narrows the manuscript to the first quarter of the thirteenth century.

notes: The inspected original title page gives 1888; a current EETS list's 1887 entry is treated as a catalog error.

extent.description: One surviving acephalous prose work represented in the U-M BODY by 60 DIV1 encoding sections, 77 paragraphs, 224 editorial notes, 75 printed-page milestones for odd pages 3-151, and 95 manuscript-page milestones. The XML also records 128 CORR, 182 ADD, 101 DEL, and 299 FOREIGN elements. The section count describes Holthausen/U-M navigation rather than a proven intrinsic medieval chapter structure; the brief closing Latin hymn is embedded prose-context material and is not encoded as an LG/L verse group.

formStatements.description: A continuous prose dialogue with embedded Latin quotations and a brief closing Latin hymn; these embedded passages do not make the work mixed prose and verse for discovery purposes.

assertion.notes: The work has homiletic affinities and uses dialogue, but Crawford cautions that it is not a sermon collection, a preaching manual, a strictly confessional text, or a conventional formal debate.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The XML has 295 LAT-tagged FOREIGN elements, including 280 in BODY prose outside notes and seven in headings. The omitted facing modern English translation is not a language of the cataloged work or digital selection.
Four GR-tagged characters occur only inside editorial NOTE1/SIC readings and are not promoted to a work-language facet.

scope.description: Biblical, liturgical, doctrinal, and other Latin quotations and the closing hymn embedded throughout the Middle English prose

notes: Current Stowe MS 240 is an unrelated nineteenth-century paper transcript volume containing early-modern state papers and correspondence; Stowe MS 240 should be used here only as a clearly labeled former shelfmark for current Stowe MS 34.
The physical witness's 49-folio current catalog extent and Crawford's 48 surviving leaves in six gatherings remain unresolved rather than silently harmonized.
The modern English translation on even pages of Holthausen's Part I belongs to the edition layer and is deliberately absent from the work-language facets for the Middle English digital selection.

next_steps: Consult the full Gunn chapter and later work-specific scholarship.
Distinguish authorial language from the localized hands and corrections of Stowe MS 34.
Do not adopt Waltham or a secular-canon author without direct evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can current linguistic, source, and historical scholarship narrow the work's composition date and place beyond approximately 1150-1200 in South-East England?

next_steps: Review source analysis and analogues without reconstructing lost text as though witnessed.
Search for quotations or derivative excerpts that could preserve material from the lost opening.
Continue labeling the surviving unit acephalous.
openQuestions.question: What did the missing initial eight-leaf quire contain, and did it preserve the original incipit, title, and an earlier treatment of confession or principal sins?

next_steps: Compare the section headings and divisions against a complete manuscript facsimile when one becomes available.
Consult Crawford's full transcription and rubrication discussion.
Until then, expose sixty as encoding/editorial navigation rather than canonical chapters.
openQuestions.question: Which, if any, of Holthausen's and U-M's sixty section divisions reflect intrinsic manuscript rubrics or stable medieval textual divisions?

notes: This broad attribution does not adopt the proposed Waltham association as a composition place.
Scribal dialect does not by itself prove the author's place of composition.
placeStatements.placeLabel: Western or south-western Essex and the Essex-London-Middlesex area

scope.description: Language of Stowe MS 34's principal and secondary hands

notes: A secular canon and a Waltham setting have been proposed, but the evidence is too speculative to name or characterize the author.
This is a transmission-layer responsibility, not authorship of the medieval work.

scope.description: The 1888 Part I Middle English text and facing translation

summaries.value: An anonymous early Middle English prose dialogue in which the sinful Soul is awakened and instructed by Reason, with the Body also entering the moral conversation. The surviving text examines sorrow and sloth, pride and disobedience, perjury, lying, backbiting, fraud, impatience, self-will, injustice, the senses, faith, hope, charity, humility, fear, mercy, wisdom, obedience, confession, restraint, prayer, and related vices and virtues. Its instruction draws heavily on Latin biblical and liturgical quotation and closes with Veni creator spiritus.

The work is known from one acephalous manuscript, current shelfmark British Library Stowe MS 34 and former shelfmark Stowe MS 240. The first eight-leaf quire is lost. The surviving work occupies fols. 1r-48r; Holthausen and U-M map it as manuscript pages 1-95. Current catalog and scholarly descriptions disagree between 49 cataloged folios and Crawford's 48 surviving leaves, so the discrepancy remains explicit rather than being normalized away.

The University of Michigan artifact is a selection from the 1967 OUP reprint of Holthausen's 1888 Part I. It encodes the Middle English text on odd printed pages 3-151 and three reprint title pages, while omitting Holthausen's facing modern English translation on even pages 2-150. Its sixty DIV1 containers and headings are edition/encoding navigation and are not asserted as sixty authorial or canonical chapters.

notes: The conventional title is editorial; the surviving witness begins mid-sentence after the loss of its first quire.
Part I: Text and Translation is edition-level carrier wording and is not included in the intellectual-work title.
titles.value: Vices and Virtues: A Soul's Confession of its Sins with Reason's Description of the Virtues: A Middle-English Dialogue of About 1200 A.D.

notes: This is a mid-sentence extant opening, not the lost original incipit.

scope.description: First surviving words in the U-M/Holthausen text

notes: Holthausen then prints the Latin hymn incipit Veni creator spiritus as a separate final paragraph; it is not substituted for the prose work's explicit.

scope.description: Final sentence of the prose conclusion

titles.value: Alswa swa we habbeð niede him to bidden be daiȝ and be nihte, and ofte and ȝelome, alswa hit is niede him to heriȝen. Amen.

work.summary: An anonymous early Middle English prose dialogue in which the sinful Soul is awakened and instructed by Reason, with the Body also entering the moral conversation. The surviving text examines sorrow and sloth, pride and disobedience, perjury, lying, backbiting, fraud, impatience, self-will, injustice, the senses, faith, hope, charity, humility, fear, mercy, wisdom, obedience, confession, restraint, prayer, and related vices and virtues. Its instruction draws heavily on Latin biblical and liturgical quotation and closes with Veni creator spiritus.
